# One-way ANOVA {#sec-ch3}




## Introduction {#sec-ch3-intro}

In @sec-ch1, we saw that comparing two groups with a $t$-test is equivalent to simple regression with a dummy variable. What happens when we have **three or more groups**? We cannot use a single numeric variable to represent group membership (e.g., coding Basal = 1, DRTA = 2, Strat = 3) because that would impose a linear ordering and equal spacing that may not exist.

The solution is to use **multiple dummy variables** --- one for each group minus one (the reference group). This is the one-way Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) model, which is simply a multiple regression model where all predictors are dummy variables representing group membership.

This chapter makes a key insight explicit: **ANOVA is regression**. The $F$-test in ANOVA, the pairwise comparisons, the effect sizes --- all of these are special cases of the regression framework we have been building.

::: {.callout-note}
## From $t$-test to ANOVA
| Scenario | Groups | Dummies | Test |
|:---------|:-------|:--------|:-----|
| @sec-ch1 | 2 | 1 | $t$-test ($F$ with $df_1 = 1$) |
| This chapter | $k+1$ | $k$ | $F$-test with $df_1 = k$ |

When $k = 1$ (two groups), the ANOVA $F$-statistic equals $t^2$, and the $p$-values are identical. One-way ANOVA is the natural generalization of the two-sample $t$-test.
:::

::: {.objectives}
[Learning Objectives]{.obj-title}

After completing this chapter, you will be able to:

1. Specify a one-way ANOVA model using dummy variables
2. Interpret dummy variable coefficients as group mean differences from a reference group
3. Construct and interpret the ANOVA table (MSS, SSE, $F$-test)
4. Conduct pairwise comparisons with appropriate corrections for multiple testing
5. Compute and interpret effect size measures (eta-squared, Cohen's $d$)
6. Explain the connection between ANOVA, regression, and the $t$-test
:::


## Motivating Example: Instruction Methods and Math Achievement {#sec-ch3-example}

We use the `instruction` dataset, which contains data on students' math achievement across different classroom settings. To illustrate one-way ANOVA, we will categorize teacher preparation level (`mathprep`) into three groups and examine whether math gains differ across preparation levels.

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"}
# Load the instruction dataset
data(instruction)

# Create a 3-level factor for teacher math preparation
instruction <- instruction |>
  mutate(
    prep_level = case_when(
      mathprep <= 3  ~ "Low",
      mathprep <= 4  ~ "Medium",
      mathprep > 4   ~ "High"
    ),
    prep_level = factor(prep_level, levels = c("Low", "Medium", "High"))
  ) |>
  filter(!is.na(mathgain), !is.na(prep_level))

# Summary by group
instruction |>
  group_by(prep_level) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    mean_gain = mean(mathgain),
    sd_gain   = sd(mathgain),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  kable(digits = 2, col.names = c("Preparation Level", "n", "Mean Math Gain", "SD"))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}


|Preparation Level |   n| Mean Math Gain|    SD|
|:-----------------|---:|--------------:|-----:|
|Low               | 926|          56.08| 34.32|
|Medium            | 169|          63.57| 34.79|
|High              |  95|          61.35| 36.08|


:::
:::

## ANOVA as Regression with Dummy Variables {#sec-ch3-model}

### Dummy Variable Coding

With three groups (Low, Medium, High) and Low as the reference group, we need **two** dummy variables:

$$
d_{Mi} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if student } i \text{ has Medium prep} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
\qquad
d_{Hi} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if student } i \text{ has High prep} \\ 0 & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

| Group | $d_{Mi}$ | $d_{Hi}$ |
|:------|:---------|:---------|
| Low (reference) | 0 | 0 |
| Medium | 1 | 0 |
| High | 0 | 1 |

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"}
set.seed(640)
sample_idx <- instruction |>
  group_by(prep_level) |>
  slice_sample(n = 4) |>
  ungroup()

mm <- model.matrix(~ prep_level, data = sample_idx) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  mutate(obs = row_number(), Group = sample_idx$prep_level) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = -c(obs, Group), names_to = "Variable", values_to = "Value") |>
  mutate(Variable = factor(Variable,
    levels = c("(Intercept)", "prep_levelMedium", "prep_levelHigh"),
    labels = c("Intercept", "d_Medium", "d_High")))

ggplot(mm, aes(x = Variable, y = reorder(factor(obs), -obs), fill = factor(Value))) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Value), fontface = "bold", size = 5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("0" = "grey90", "1" = ber640_colors["accent"]), guide = "none") +
  facet_grid(Group ~ ., scales = "free_y", space = "free_y", switch = "y") +
  labs(title = "Design Matrix: What R Creates Under the Hood",
       subtitle = "model.matrix(~ prep_level)", x = "Model Term", y = NULL) +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_blank(), axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
        strip.placement = "outside", strip.text.y.left = element_text(angle = 0, face = "bold"))
```

- **Intercept column**: Every observation has a 1 in this column, meaning every student gets the baseline (reference group) mean as a starting point
- **Low group rows**: All dummies are 0 --- these students' predicted value is just the intercept ($\alpha$)

```{.r .cell-code}
ggplot(mm, aes(x = Variable, y = reorder(factor(obs), -obs), fill = factor(Value))) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Value), fontface = "bold", size = 5) +

  facet_grid(Group ~ ., scales = "free_y", space = "free_y", switch = "y") +
```

:::

- `model.matrix(~ prep_level)` extracts the actual design matrix R uses internally when fitting the model
- Each row represents one observation; the columns are the intercept (always 1) and the dummy variables
- The reference group (Low) has all dummy columns set to 0 --- only the intercept column is 1
- `facet_grid()` groups rows by preparation level so you can see the pattern clearly


:::

The one-way ANOVA model is:

$$
y_i = \alpha + \beta_1 d_{Mi} + \beta_2 d_{Hi} + \epsilon_i
$$ {#eq-ch3-anova}

### Interpreting the Coefficients

| Group | $\text{E}(y_i)$ | In terms of group means |
|:------|:-----------------|:-----------------------|
| Low | $\alpha$ | $\mu_{\text{Low}}$ |
| Medium | $\alpha + \beta_1$ | $\mu_{\text{Medium}}$ |
| High | $\alpha + \beta_2$ | $\mu_{\text{High}}$ |

Therefore:

- $\alpha = \mu_{\text{Low}}$ (mean for the reference group)
- $\beta_1 = \mu_{\text{Medium}} - \mu_{\text{Low}}$ (Medium vs Low difference)
- $\beta_2 = \mu_{\text{High}} - \mu_{\text{Low}}$ (High vs Low difference)

::: {.callout-tip}
## General Rule for Dummy Variables
A factor with $k + 1$ levels requires $k$ dummy variables. The intercept always represents the mean of the **reference group** (the group without its own dummy). Each dummy coefficient represents the difference between that group's mean and the reference group mean.
:::

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
## Concept Check
Suppose the regression output gives: Intercept = 50.3, $\hat{\beta}_{\text{Medium}}$ = 8.7, $\hat{\beta}_{\text{High}}$ = 15.2.

1. What is the predicted mean math gain for the Low group?
2. What is the predicted mean for the Medium group?
3. Which group has the highest predicted mean?

*Answers: (1) 50.3; (2) 50.3 + 8.7 = 59.0; (3) High, with 50.3 + 15.2 = 65.5*
:::

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"}
# Parameters
model_parameters(model_anova) |> print_md()
```

|:-------------------|:-----------:|:----:|:--------------:|:-------:|:------:|
|(Intercept)         |       56.08 | 1.13 | (53.86, 58.31) |   49.43 | < .001 |

- **Intercept**: The estimated mean math gain for the Low preparation group (reference category). This equals the sample mean of the Low group exactly
- **prep_levelMedium**: The estimated difference in mean math gain between Medium and Low preparation teachers' students. A positive value means Medium-prep teachers' students gained more on average
- **prep_levelHigh**: The estimated difference in mean math gain between High and Low preparation teachers' students
- **SE**: Standard errors reflect uncertainty in each estimate. Larger groups yield smaller SEs
- **$p$-values**: Test whether each group differs significantly from the reference (Low). Note: these are *not* corrected for multiple comparisons --- see @sec-ch3-pairwise for proper pairwise tests
- To obtain the estimated mean for Medium, add the Medium coefficient to the intercept; similarly for High

### Code Explanation

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}

model_anova <- lm(mathgain ~ prep_level, data = instruction)

# Parameters
model_parameters(model_anova) |> print_md()
```
:::

- `lm(mathgain ~ prep_level)`: R automatically creates dummy variables for the factor `prep_level`, using the first level (Low) as the reference
- The intercept estimates the mean math gain for the Low preparation group
- `prep_levelMedium` estimates the Medium − Low difference
- `prep_levelHigh` estimates the High − Low difference
- `print_md()` formats the output as a clean markdown table

- **Data Mean vs Regression Mean**: The two columns should match exactly (to rounding). This is not a coincidence --- in one-way ANOVA, the OLS estimates *are* the group means
- **Why this matters**: It proves that the dummy variable regression is doing nothing more than estimating group means. The intercept $\alpha$ captures the reference group mean, and each $\beta_k$ captures the deviation of group $k$ from the reference
- This equivalence breaks down once you add covariates (ANCOVA, @sec-ch2), because the regression then estimates *adjusted* means that control for the covariates

## Coding Schemes {#sec-ch3-coding}

The dummy (treatment) coding described above is the default in R. However, other coding schemes exist:

| Scheme | Reference | Coefficients Represent |
|:-------|:----------|:----------------------|
| **Dummy** (treatment) | First/last group | Difference from reference |
| **Effects** (deviation) | Grand mean | Deviation from grand mean |
| **Helmert** | Previous groups | Difference from mean of previous groups |

::: {.callout-note}
## All Coding Schemes Give the Same ANOVA Table
The choice of coding scheme changes the interpretation of individual coefficients, but the overall $F$-test, $R^2$, and model predictions are identical. Choose the scheme that matches your research question.
:::

::: {.callout-warning}
## When to Categorize a Continuous Variable
In this chapter, we converted continuous `mathprep` into three groups for pedagogical purposes. In practice:

- **Categorize** when groups are naturally defined (treatment conditions, school types) or when the relationship is genuinely non-linear with plateau regions
- **Keep continuous** when the variable has a smooth relationship with the outcome --- categorizing discards information and reduces statistical power

See @sec-ch4 for modeling continuous predictors directly, and @sec-ch6 for handling non-linear relationships without categorizing.
:::


## The ANOVA Table {#sec-ch3-anova-table}

### Decomposing Variability

Just as in @sec-ch1-r2, we decompose the total variability into between-group and within-group components:

| Source | SS | df | MS = SS/df |
|:-------|:---|:---|:-----------|
| Model (between groups) | MSS | $k$ | MMS |
| Error (within groups) | SSE | $n - k - 1$ | MSE |
| Total | TSS | $n - 1$ | |

In one-way ANOVA:

- **MSS** (Between-Group SS): $\sum_i (\hat{y}_i - \bar{y})^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k+1} n_j (\bar{y}_j - \bar{y})^2$
- **SSE** (Within-Group SS): $\sum_i (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2 = \sum_{j=1}^{k+1} \sum_{i \in j} (y_i - \bar{y}_j)^2$
- **MSE** is the **pooled within-group variance**

### The $F$-test

The null hypothesis is that all group means are equal:

$$
H_0: \beta_1 = \beta_2 = \cdots = \beta_k = 0 \quad (\mu_1 = \mu_2 = \cdots = \mu_{k+1})
$$

$$
F = \frac{\text{MMS}}{\text{MSE}} \sim F(k, \; n - k - 1) \text{ under } H_0
$$ {#eq-ch3-ftest}

- **Sum Sq (prep_level)**: The between-group sum of squares (MSS) --- variability in math gains attributable to differences among preparation levels
- **Sum Sq (Residuals)**: The within-group sum of squares (SSE) --- variability among students within the same preparation level
- **Mean Sq**: SS divided by degrees of freedom. The ratio of Mean Sq (model) to Mean Sq (residuals) gives the $F$-statistic
- **$F$ value**: A large $F$ means the between-group variability is large relative to within-group variability, providing evidence that group means differ
- **Pr(>F)**: If this $p$-value is below the significance level (e.g., 0.05), we reject $H_0$ that all group means are equal. Follow up with pairwise comparisons (@sec-ch3-pairwise) to identify which specific groups differ

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"}
  geom_area(data = filter(f_df, x >= f_crit),

  geom_vline(xintercept = f_obs, linetype = "dashed",

  annotate("text", x = f_obs, y = max(f_df$density) * 0.7,
           label = paste0("F_obs = ", round(f_obs, 2)),
           hjust = -0.1, color = ber640_colors["accent"], fontface = "bold") +
  labs(title = bquote(F(.(df1)*","~.(df2))~"Distribution Under"~H[0]),
       subtitle = expression("Shaded = rejection region at "*alpha*" = 0.05"),
       x = "F-statistic", y = "Density")
```

```{ojs}
    .map((y, i) => ({ y, group: "Group 3", id: i }));
  return g1.concat(g2).concat(g3);
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// --- ANOVA computations ---
anova_stats = {
  const groups = ["Group 1", "Group 2", "Group 3"];
  const N = anova_data.length;
  const k = groups.length;

  // Grand mean
  const grandMean = anova_data.reduce((s, d) => s + d.y, 0) / N;

  // Group means
  const groupMeans = {};
  const groupData = {};
  for (const g of groups) {
    const vals = anova_data.filter(d => d.group === g).map(d => d.y);
    groupMeans[g] = vals.reduce((s, v) => s + v, 0) / vals.length;
    groupData[g] = vals;
  }

  // SSB = sum n_k * (ybar_k - ybar)^2
  let SSB = 0;
  for (const g of groups) {
    const diff = groupMeans[g] - grandMean;
    SSB += groupData[g].length * diff * diff;
  }

  // SSW = sum sum (y_ij - ybar_j)^2
  let SSW = 0;
  for (const g of groups) {
    for (const v of groupData[g]) {
      const diff = v - groupMeans[g];
      SSW += diff * diff;
    }
  }

  const SST = SSB + SSW;
  const dfB = k - 1;
  const dfW = N - k;
  const MSB = SSB / dfB;
  const MSW = SSW / dfW;
  const F = MSB / MSW;
  const etaSq = SSB / SST;

  // p-value approximation using regularized incomplete beta function
  // F ~ F(dfB, dfW)  =>  p = 1 - I_x(dfB/2, dfW/2) where x = dfB*F/(dfB*F + dfW)
  const x = (dfB * F) / (dfB * F + dfW);
  const pValue = 1 - regBetaI(x, dfB / 2, dfW / 2);

  return {
    grandMean, groupMeans, SSB, SSW, SST,
    dfB, dfW, MSB, MSW, F, etaSq, pValue,
    groups
  };
}

// Regularized incomplete beta function (continued fraction approx)
function regBetaI(x, a, b) {
  if (x <= 0) return 0;
  if (x >= 1) return 1;

  // Use the log-beta for normalization
  const lbeta = lgamma(a) + lgamma(b) - lgamma(a + b);
  const front = Math.exp(Math.log(x) * a + Math.log(1 - x) * b - lbeta) / a;

  // Lentz's continued fraction
  const maxIter = 200;
  const eps = 1e-14;
  let f = 1, c = 1, d = 0;

  for (let m = 0; m <= maxIter; m++) {
    let numerator;
    if (m === 0) {
      numerator = 1;
    } else {
      const k = m;
      const isOdd = k % 2 === 1;
      const m2 = Math.floor((k - 1) / 2) + (isOdd ? 0 : 0);
      if (isOdd) {
        const mi = Math.floor(k / 2);
        numerator = -(a + mi) * (a + b + mi) * x /
                     ((a + 2 * mi) * (a + 2 * mi + 1));
      } else {
        const mi = k / 2;
        numerator = mi * (b - mi) * x /
                     ((a + 2 * mi - 1) * (a + 2 * mi));
      }
    }

    d = 1 + numerator * d;
    if (Math.abs(d) < eps) d = eps;
    d = 1 / d;
    c = 1 + numerator / c;
    if (Math.abs(c) < eps) c = eps;
    f *= c * d;
    if (Math.abs(c * d - 1) < eps && m > 0) break;
  }

  return front * (f - 1);
}

function lgamma(x) {
  // Lanczos approximation
  const g = 7;
  const coef = [
    0.99999999999980993, 676.5203681218851, -1259.1392167224028,
    771.32342877765313, -176.61502916214059, 12.507343278686905,
    -0.13857109526572012, 9.9843695780195716e-6, 1.5056327351493116e-7
  ];
  if (x < 0.5) {
    return Math.log(Math.PI / Math.sin(Math.PI * x)) - lgamma(1 - x);
  }
  x -= 1;
  let a = coef[0];
  const t = x + g + 0.5;
  for (let i = 1; i < coef.length; i++) {
    a += coef[i] / (x + i);
  }
  return 0.5 * Math.log(2 * Math.PI) + (x + 0.5) * Math.log(t) - t + Math.log(a);
}
```

## Checking ANOVA Assumptions {#sec-ch3-assumptions}

Three key assumptions underlie the ANOVA $F$-test:

1. **Independence**: Observations are independent (addressed by study design)
2. **Normality**: Residuals within each group are approximately normal
3. **Homogeneity of variance**: All groups have equal population variances ($\sigma^2_1 = \sigma^2_2 = \cdots$)

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"}
check_model(model_anova, check = c("normality", "homogeneity"))
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![Diagnostic plots for the ANOVA model: normality of residuals (Q-Q plot) and homogeneity of variance.](03-one-way-anova_files/figure-html/fig-ch3-assumptions-1.png){#fig-ch3-assumptions fig-alt='Multi-panel diagnostic figure produced by the performance package for the ANOVA model. The top panel is a quantile-quantile (Q-Q) plot comparing standardized residuals on the y-axis to theoretical normal quantiles on the x-axis, assessing the normality assumption. The bottom panel displays residual spread across fitted values or groups to assess homogeneity of variance. Deviations from the reference line in the Q-Q plot or unequal spread across groups indicate potential assumption violations.' width=2400}
:::
:::


![](03-one-way-anova_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-31-1.png){width=2400}

- **Q-Q plot (normality)**: If the points closely follow the reference line, the normality assumption is reasonable. Systematic deviations at the tails indicate skewness or heavy tails
- **Homogeneity plot**: Look for roughly equal vertical spread across the fitted values (group means). A funnel shape (wider spread for some groups) suggests unequal variances
- These are informal visual checks --- use Levene's test below for a formal test of equal variances

### Code Explanation

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
check_model(model_anova, check = c("normality", "homogeneity"))
```
:::

- `check_model()` from the `performance` package produces visual diagnostics for regression/ANOVA models
- `check = c("normality", "homogeneity")` selects two key diagnostic plots:
  - **Normality**: A Q-Q plot of residuals; points should fall along the diagonal line
  - **Homogeneity**: A plot of residuals vs. fitted values; spread should be roughly equal across groups


:::

- **$F$ value**: The test statistic for the equality of variances across groups
- **Pr(>F)**: If this $p$-value is below 0.05, we reject the null of equal variances
- A non-significant result means we do **not** have evidence of unequal variances (the assumption is reasonable)
- A significant result suggests the ANOVA $F$-test may be unreliable, especially if group sizes differ substantially

### Code Explanation

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Formal test for equal variances (Levene's test)
car::leveneTest(mathgain ~ prep_level, data = instruction)
```
:::

- `car::leveneTest()` performs Levene's test for homogeneity of variance
- The null hypothesis is that all groups have equal population variances
- Levene's test is robust to non-normality because it uses deviations from the group medians (by default)


:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## Robustness of the F-test
The $F$-test is fairly robust to mild violations of normality, especially with large samples (Central Limit Theorem). However, it is **sensitive to unequal variances** when group sizes differ substantially. If Levene's test is significant, consider Welch's ANOVA:

`oneway.test(mathgain ~ prep_level, data = instruction, var.equal = FALSE)`
:::


## Pairwise Comparisons and Multiple Testing {#sec-ch3-pairwise}

### The Multiple Testing Problem

If the overall $F$-test is significant, we want to know *which* groups differ. With 3 groups, there are $\binom{3}{2} = 3$ pairwise comparisons. With more groups, the number of tests grows quickly, and the probability of at least one false positive (Type I error) inflates:

| Groups | Pairwise tests | $P(\text{at least 1 false positive})$ |
|:-------|:---------------|:--------------------------------------|
| 3 | 3 | 0.14 |
| 6 | 15 | 0.54 |
| 11 | 55 | 0.94 |

::: {.panel-tabset}
### Output

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code  code-fold="true" code-summary="Show R Code"}
k_groups <- 2:15
n_tests <- choose(k_groups, 2)
fwer_none <- 1 - (1 - 0.05)^n_tests
fwer_bonf <- pmin(1, 1 - (1 - 0.05 / n_tests)^n_tests)

mt_df <- tibble(
  Groups = rep(k_groups, 2),
  FWER = c(fwer_none, fwer_bonf),
  Method = rep(c("No Correction", "Bonferroni"), each = length(k_groups))
)

ggplot(mt_df, aes(x = Groups, y = FWER, color = Method)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) + geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.05, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  annotate("text", x = 14, y = 0.08, label = "α = 0.05", color = "grey50") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("No Correction" = unname(ber640_colors["secondary"]),
                                "Bonferroni" = unname(ber640_colors["accent"]))) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = "The Multiple Testing Problem",
       subtitle = "Family-wise error rate by number of groups",
       x = "Number of Groups", y = "P(At Least One False Positive)")
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](03-one-way-anova_files/figure-html/unnamed-chunk-35-1.png){width=2400}
:::
:::

- **Red line (No Correction)**: The family-wise error rate rises sharply as the number of groups increases. With just 6 groups (15 pairwise tests), the chance of at least one false positive exceeds 50%
- **Blue line (Bonferroni)**: The Bonferroni correction keeps the family-wise error rate near or below 0.05, regardless of how many groups are compared
- **Takeaway**: Without correction, conducting many pairwise tests is virtually guaranteed to produce false positives. This is why post-hoc correction methods are essential in exploratory analyses

### Code Explanation

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
k_groups <- 2:15
n_tests <- choose(k_groups, 2)
fwer_none <- 1 - (1 - 0.05)^n_tests
fwer_bonf <- pmin(1, 1 - (1 - 0.05 / n_tests)^n_tests)

mt_df <- tibble(
  Groups = rep(k_groups, 2),
  FWER = c(fwer_none, fwer_bonf),
  Method = rep(c("No Correction", "Bonferroni"), each = length(k_groups))
)

ggplot(mt_df, aes(x = Groups, y = FWER, color = Method)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) + geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.05, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +
  annotate("text", x = 14, y = 0.08, label = "α = 0.05", color = "grey50") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("No Correction" = unname(ber640_colors["secondary"]),
                                "Bonferroni" = unname(ber640_colors["accent"]))) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent_format(), limits = c(0, 1)) +
  labs(title = "The Multiple Testing Problem",
       subtitle = "Family-wise error rate by number of groups",
       x = "Number of Groups", y = "P(At Least One False Positive)")
```
:::

- `choose(k_groups, 2)` computes the number of pairwise comparisons for each group count
- `fwer_none`: the probability of at least one false positive without any correction, using $1 - (1 - \alpha)^m$ where $m$ is the number of tests
- `fwer_bonf`: the family-wise error rate when Bonferroni correction is applied ($\alpha / m$ per test)
- The dashed line at 0.05 marks the nominal significance level


:::

### Interactive Exploration: Multiple Comparisons {.unnumbered}

::: {.callout-tip collapse="false"}
## Try It: The Multiple Testing Problem

When comparing multiple groups, each pairwise test carries a 5% false positive risk. As the number of comparisons grows, the probability that *at least one* test falsely rejects H₀ increases dramatically. Run simulations to see how correction methods like Bonferroni and Holm control this family-wise error rate.
:::

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
import { ber640 as mc_ber640, withAlpha as mc_withAlpha,
         themeColors as mc_themeColors, plotDefaults as mc_plotDefaults,
         normalRandom as mc_normalRandom, formatCoef as mc_formatCoef } from "../ojs/shared.js"
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

viewof mc_method = Inputs.radio(["None", "Bonferroni", "Holm"], {value: "None", label: "Correction method"})

viewof mc_run = Inputs.button("Run 100 Simulations")
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
mc_nPerGroup = 20
mc_alpha = 0.05
mc_nSims = 100
mc_nComparisons = mc_nGroups * (mc_nGroups - 1) / 2
mc_expectedRate = 1 - Math.pow(1 - mc_alpha, mc_nComparisons)

// Simple seeded PRNG (mulberry32)
function mc_mulberry32(seed) {
  return function() {
    seed |= 0; seed = seed + 0x6D2B79F5 | 0;
    let t = Math.imul(seed ^ seed >>> 15, 1 | seed);
    t = t + Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 7, 61 | t) ^ t;
    return ((t ^ t >>> 14) >>> 0) / 4294967296;
  }
}

function mc_normalSample(rng) {
  const u1 = rng();
  const u2 = rng();
  return Math.sqrt(-2 * Math.log(u1)) * Math.cos(2 * Math.PI * u2);
}

function mc_generateGroup(n, rng) {
  const data = [];
  for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) data.push(mc_normalSample(rng));
  return data;
}

function mc_mean(arr) {
  let s = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) s += arr[i];
  return s / arr.length;
}

function mc_variance(arr, m) {
  let s = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < arr.length; i++) s += (arr[i] - m) * (arr[i] - m);
  return s / (arr.length - 1);
}

function mc_tTestPValue(g1, g2) {
  const m1 = mc_mean(g1), m2 = mc_mean(g2);
  const v1 = mc_variance(g1, m1), v2 = mc_variance(g2, m2);
  const n1 = g1.length, n2 = g2.length;
  const sp2 = ((n1 - 1) * v1 + (n2 - 1) * v2) / (n1 + n2 - 2);
  const se = Math.sqrt(sp2 * (1 / n1 + 1 / n2));
  if (se < 1e-12) return 1;
  const t = Math.abs(m1 - m2) / se;
  return 2 * mc_normalSurvival(t);
}

function mc_normalSurvival(x) {
  if (x < 0) return 1 - mc_normalSurvival(-x);
  const b0 = 0.2316419;
  const b1 = 0.319381530, b2 = -0.356563782, b3 = 1.781477937;
  const b4 = -1.821255978, b5 = 1.330274429;
  const t = 1 / (1 + b0 * x);
  const phi = Math.exp(-x * x / 2) / Math.sqrt(2 * Math.PI);
  return phi * t * (b1 + t * (b2 + t * (b3 + t * (b4 + t * b5))));
}

function mc_bonferroniAdjust(pvals, nComp) {
  return pvals.map(p => Math.min(p * nComp, 1));
}

function mc_holmAdjust(pvals) {
  const n = pvals.length;
  const indexed = pvals.map((p, i) => ({p, i}));
  indexed.sort((a, b) => a.p - b.p);
  const adjusted = new Array(n);
  let cumMax = 0;
  for (let j = 0; j < n; j++) {
    const adj = indexed[j].p * (n - j);
    cumMax = Math.max(cumMax, adj);
    adjusted[indexed[j].i] = Math.min(cumMax, 1);
  }
  return adjusted;
}
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
mc_simResults = {
  mc_run;  // depend on button clicks for reactivity

  const k = mc_nGroups;
  const n = mc_nPerGroup;
  const method = mc_method;
  const baseSeed = Date.now();

  let falsePositiveCount = 0;
  const simDetails = [];

  for (let sim = 0; sim < mc_nSims; sim++) {
    const rng = mc_mulberry32(baseSeed + sim * 1000);
    const groups = [];
    for (let g = 0; g < k; g++) groups.push(mc_generateGroup(n, rng));

    const pvals = [];
    for (let i = 0; i < k - 1; i++) {
      for (let j = i + 1; j < k; j++) {
        pvals.push(mc_tTestPValue(groups[i], groups[j]));
      }
    }

    let adjustedP;
    if (method === "Bonferroni") adjustedP = mc_bonferroniAdjust(pvals, pvals.length);
    else if (method === "Holm") adjustedP = mc_holmAdjust(pvals);
    else adjustedP = pvals.slice();

    const anySignificant = adjustedP.some(p => p < mc_alpha);
    if (anySignificant) falsePositiveCount++;
    simDetails.push({ sim: sim + 1, anyFalse: anySignificant });
  }

  return { falsePositiveCount, observedRate: falsePositiveCount / mc_nSims, simDetails };
}

mc_correctedAlpha = {
  if (mc_method === "Bonferroni") return mc_alpha / mc_nComparisons;
  if (mc_method === "Holm") return mc_alpha / mc_nComparisons;
  return mc_alpha;
}
```

::: {.callout-note}
## Exploratory vs Confirmatory Approaches
| Approach | When to use | Multiple testing? |
|:---------|:-----------|:-----------------|
| **Exploratory** | No specific hypotheses about groups | Yes --- use Bonferroni, Tukey, or Scheffé |
| **Confirmatory** | Planned comparisons based on theory | No correction needed for a small number of planned tests |

When you have specific hypotheses before looking at the data (e.g., "new methods will outperform the standard"), use planned comparisons. When the comparisons are suggested by the data, use post-hoc corrections.
:::


## Effect Size Measures {#sec-ch3-effect-size}

Statistical significance depends on sample size --- with a large enough sample, even trivial differences become "significant." Effect size measures provide a standardized way to assess practical importance.

:::

- **$\eta^2$**: The proportion of total variance in math gains explained by teacher preparation level. Use Cohen's benchmarks to gauge practical significance: small (.01), medium (.06), large (.14)
- **90% CI**: The confidence interval for $\eta^2$. Effect sizes are inherently positive, so a 90% CI is conventional (corresponding to a one-sided test). If the lower bound is near zero, the effect may not be practically meaningful despite statistical significance
- Unlike $p$-values, effect sizes do not depend on sample size, making them essential for judging practical importance in education research

### Code Explanation

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Eta-squared and partial eta-squared
effectsize::eta_squared(model_anova)
```
:::

- `effectsize::eta_squared()` computes eta-squared and its confidence interval from the ANOVA model
- $\eta^2 = \text{MSS} / \text{TSS}$ is the proportion of variance explained by group membership (equals $R^2$ in one-way ANOVA)
- **Cohen's guidelines**: $\eta^2 = .01$ (small), $.06$ (medium), $.14$ (large)


:::

::: {.callout-tip}
## $\eta^2$ vs $R^2$
In one-way ANOVA, $\eta^2 = R^2$ exactly. In factorial ANOVA or ANCOVA, **partial** $\eta^2$ is more commonly reported, which measures the proportion of variance explained by a factor *after removing* the variance explained by other factors.
:::

::: {.callout-note}
## Interpreting Effect Sizes in Education Research
Cohen's benchmarks are generic guidelines. In education contexts:

- **Small effects** ($d \approx 0.2$, $\eta^2 \approx .01$) are common for instructional interventions and can be meaningful if the intervention is low-cost or scalable
- **Medium effects** ($d \approx 0.5$, $\eta^2 \approx .06$) are typical for well-designed, targeted programs
- **Large effects** ($d \approx 0.8$, $\eta^2 \approx .14$) are rare for classroom-level interventions

To convert a pairwise ANOVA comparison to Cohen's $d$:
$$d = \frac{\bar{y}_j - \bar{y}_k}{\sqrt{MSE}}$$
where $\sqrt{MSE}$ is the pooled within-group SD from the ANOVA table.
:::


## Summary and Key Takeaways {#sec-ch3-summary}

::: {.callout-important}
## Key Takeaways

1. **ANOVA is regression** with dummy variables. A factor with $k + 1$ levels requires $k$ dummies.
2. The **intercept** is the reference group mean; each **dummy coefficient** is the difference between that group and the reference group
3. The **$F$-test** tests whether *any* group means differ ($H_0$: all means are equal)
4. **Post-hoc pairwise comparisons** require correction for multiple testing (e.g., Tukey, Bonferroni) when the comparisons are exploratory
5. **Planned comparisons** based on theory do not require correction and should not be preceded by the overall $F$-test
6. **Effect sizes** ($\eta^2$, Cohen's $d$) complement $p$-values by quantifying practical importance
7. The progression: $t$-test (2 groups) $\rightarrow$ one-way ANOVA (3+ groups) $\rightarrow$ ANCOVA (groups + covariates) --- all are special cases of the general linear model
:::


## Exercises {#sec-ch3-exercises}

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
## Exercise 3.1: Reference Group Selection
Refit the ANOVA model using "High" as the reference group. How do the coefficients change? Does $R^2$ change?

**Hint**: Use `relevel(prep_level, ref = "High")` to change the reference group.
:::

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
## Exercise 3.2: Post-hoc Comparisons
Which specific pairwise comparisons are statistically significant at the 5% level? Which correction method did you use, and why?

**Hint**: Use `avg_comparisons(model_anova, variables = list(prep_level = "pairwise"))` for unadjusted pairwise comparisons. For Bonferroni correction, multiply each $p$-value by the number of comparisons (3). Consider when Bonferroni vs. Tukey is more appropriate.
:::

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
## Exercise 3.3: Adding a Covariate
Extend the one-way ANOVA model by adding `ses` as a covariate (making it an ANCOVA model). How does controlling for SES change the group differences? Compare $R^2$ to the ANOVA-only model.

**Hint**: `lm(mathgain ~ prep_level + ses, data = instruction)`. Compare both `r2(model_anova)` and `r2(model_ancova)`. Think about what changes in the coefficients mean substantively: does the preparation-level effect shrink, grow, or stay the same after adjusting for SES?
:::

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
## Exercise 3.4: Why Not Use Multiple t-tests?
A colleague proposes testing all pairwise group differences using separate $t$-tests (Low vs Medium, Low vs High, Medium vs High) instead of ANOVA.

(a) What is the family-wise Type I error rate if each test uses $\alpha = 0.05$?
(b) How does the ANOVA $F$-test address this problem?
(c) When would planned comparisons (without correction) be appropriate?

**Hint**: Review the multiple testing table and @fig-ch3-multiple-testing. For part (c), think about pre-specified hypotheses vs. data-driven exploration.
:::

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
## Exercise 3.5: What Does a Significant F-test Actually Tell You?
A one-way ANOVA comparing math gains across three preparation levels yields $F(2, 897) = 4.53$, $p = 0.011$. A colleague concludes that "all three groups differ significantly from each other." Is this interpretation correct? What exactly does a significant $F$-test tell you, and what does it not tell you? What additional analysis is needed to determine which specific groups differ?

**Hint**: The omnibus $F$-test only tells you that at least one group mean differs from at least one other. It does not identify which pairs differ. You need post-hoc pairwise comparisons (e.g., Tukey's HSD or Bonferroni-corrected $t$-tests) to determine specific group differences.
:::

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
## Exercise 3.6: Choosing a Reference Category
You are modeling salary differences across four departments (Engineering, Marketing, HR, Finance) using dummy coding. Your colleague coded Engineering as the reference group and found that Marketing has a significant positive coefficient while HR and Finance do not. If you re-ran the model with Marketing as the reference group instead, which results would change and which would stay the same? Specifically, would the overall $F$-test, $R^2$, or the predicted group means change?

**Hint**: Changing the reference category changes the individual dummy coefficients and their $p$-values, but the overall $F$-test, $R^2$, and the predicted (fitted) means for each group remain identical. The model fit is the same; only the parameterization differs.
:::

::: {.callout-caution collapse="true"}
## Exercise 3.7: Assumption Violation in ANOVA
You run a one-way ANOVA and examine the residuals. The residual spread for Group A (n = 15) is roughly three times wider than for Group B (n = 150). The Levene's test for equality of variances is significant at $p = 0.002$. What assumption is violated? How does this violation affect the reliability of your $F$-test, especially given the unequal sample sizes? What alternative approach could you use?

**Hint**: This violates the homogeneity of variance (homoscedasticity) assumption. With unequal variances and unequal group sizes, the standard $F$-test can become either liberal or conservative depending on whether the larger variance is in the smaller or larger group. Consider using Welch's ANOVA (`oneway.test()` in R), which does not assume equal variances.
:::
